title.en: Unit economics structure: 5-year credit-card customer lifetime
title.es: Estructura de economía unitaria: vida útil de 5 años de un cliente de tarjeta de crédito

question.en: Walk me through the unit economics of a Capital One credit-card customer over a 5-year lifetime. Do not use real numbers — use reasonable assumptions and show your structure clearly. Flag which variables you would want to sharpen if you were actually building this model.
question.es: Explícame la economía unitaria de un cliente de tarjeta de crédito de Capital One durante 5 años. No uses números reales — usa suposiciones razonables y muestra tu estructura claramente. Señala qué variables querrías afinar si realmente estuvieras construyendo este modelo.

must_include: a structured revenue waterfall showing the main income streams in order: interchange revenue (% of spend × average monthly spend × 12 × 5), interest income (revolve rate × average balance × APR × months revolving), and fee income (annual fee if applicable, late fees — noted as declining with tenure)

a structured cost waterfall showing: acquisition cost (one-time CAC), credit loss provision (expected loss rate × average balance × tenure), fraud loss (fraud rate × spend volume), and ongoing servicing cost (per-account-year × 5)

explicit separation of one-time costs (CAC, onboarding) from recurring costs (servicing, loss provision) — flagging that CAC is incurred in year 0 and the model only becomes positive once cumulative CM exceeds CAC

at least three variables flagged as high-sensitivity with a brief reason each: revolve rate (drives interest income materially), credit loss rate (varies 3–10x between prime and subprime), and annual spend (drives interchange, the primary driver for transactors)
the payback intuition: approximate the year in which CAC is recovered from cumulative CM, showing the reasoning even if rough
must_avoid: mixing LTV (multi-year cumulative) with per-year economics without labeling which timeframe each number refers to
ignoring that customers who revolve and customers who pay in full (transactors) have completely different unit economics — the model should distinguish or at least name this bifurcation
presenting only the revenue side without costs, or only costs without revenue — unit economics requires both
